elements.sent-2411-001: An object and a task are given to the model, which are then segmented into a labeled dictionary of subparts.
elements.sent-2411-002: The relevant subpart for grasping and the task are determined by prompting a Large Language Model (LLM).
elements.sent-2411-003: Finally, Quality Diversity algorithms generate grasp candidates that are evaluated based on the distance between the gripper contact point and the relevant subparts.
elements.sent-2411-004: Proximity to the optimal grasp subpart as well as distance to the task subpart is rewarded and an optimal task-conditioned grasp is determined.. \end{strip.}
elements.sent-2411-005: Task-aware robotic grasping is a challenging problem that requires the integration of semantic understanding and geometric reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-006: Traditional grasp planning approaches focus on stable or feasible grasps, often disregarding the specific tasks the robot needs to accomplish.
elements.sent-2411-007: This paper proposes a novel framework that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) and Quality Diversity (QD) algorithms to enable zero-shot task-conditioned grasp selection.
elements.sent-2411-008: The framework segments objects into meaningful subparts and labels each subpart semantically, creating structured representations that can be used to prompt an LLM.
elements.sent-2411-009: By coupling semantic and geometric representations of an object's structure, the LLM's knowledge about tasks and which parts to grasp can be applied in the physical world.
elements.sent-2411-010: The QD-generated grasp archive provides a diverse set of grasps, allowing us to select the most suitable grasp based on the task.
elements.sent-2411-011: We evaluate the proposed method on a subset of the YCB dataset, where a Franka Emika robot is assigned to perform various actions based on object-specific task requirements.
elements.sent-2411-012: We created a ground truth by conducting a survey with six participants to determine the best grasp region for each task-object combination according to human intuition.
elements.sent-2411-013: The model was evaluated on 12 different objects across 4–7 object-specific tasks, achieving a weighted intersection over union (IoU) of 76.4\% when compared to the survey data.
elements.sent-2411-014: Robotic grasping, a fundamental aspect of manipulation, remains a complex and partially unsolved problem due to the wide range of object shapes, textures, and dynamic interaction requirements \cite{kroemer2020reviewrobotlearningmanipulation .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Beyond ensuring stability, effective grasping often requires consideration of the broader context, particularly the task that follows.
elements.sent-2411-016: In such scenarios, the grasp chosen for an object is not only influenced by its geometric properties but also by its functional requirements and the semantic understanding of its purpose.
elements.sent-2411-017: Traditional grasp planning approaches often aim to identify stable or feasible grasps, largely independent of the object's broader context or its intended use \cite{Kleeberger2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: This limitation creates an obstacle between robotic grasping capabilities and the nuanced demands of real-world applications, where the appropriateness of a grasp is inherently tied to the task that follows—such as pouring liquid from a cup, using a tool for assembly, or positioning an object for inspection.
elements.sent-2411-019: Addressing these contextual dependencies is critical to improving task-oriented robotic manipulation.
elements.sent-2411-020: Grasp synthesis—the process of generating grasps for an object—has traditionally focused on identifying a single "best" grasp, as demonstrated by frameworks like Dex-Net \cite{mahler2017dexnet and GraspNet \cite{fang2020graspnet , which prioritize stability and robustness.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: QD-Grasp \cite{huber2024speeding6dofgraspsampling introduces a novel approach to grasp synthesis by leveraging Quality Diversity algorithms to generate a diverse set of stable, high-quality grasps.}
elements.sent-2411-022: By optimizing for both diversity and stability, QD-Grasp generates an archive of numerous distinct yet reliable grasp poses.
elements.sent-2411-023: This diversity is particularly important for task-aware grasping, where selected grasps must not only ensure object stability but also accommodate functional and semantic requirements, such as how an object will be used.
elements.sent-2411-024: Tasks often necessitate specific grasp configurations that traditional methods might overlook, making a broad set of solutions essential.
elements.sent-2411-025: QD-Grasp’s ability to produce a diverse range of grasps improves the likelihood that an appropriate option for any given task is available.
elements.sent-2411-026: Our work aims to leverage this diverse grasp repertoire to find stable grasps on objects while considering the task ahead.
elements.sent-2411-027: Another important concept in task-aware grasping is the concept of affordances, which was introduced by Gibson in 1979 \cite{gibson1979ecological and refers to the actionable possibilities an environment offers to an agent, contingent on both the agent’s capabilities and the environmental properties.}
elements.sent-2411-028: In robotics, affordances play a crucial role in bridging perception with action, allowing robots to understand the functional properties of objects beyond their geometric or visual characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-029: This concept inspired deep-learning based grasping approaches like \cite{chen2022learning6doftaskorientedgrasp that can generate task-aware grasping poses for limited object categories, but unfortunately lacks generalizability.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Another approach to using affordances within robotics is to localize them on objects and then use that information for grasping or manipulation.
elements.sent-2411-031: The earliest works in affordance localization used handcrafted geometric features \cite{myers2015affordance .}
elements.sent-2411-032: With the emergence of deep learning models, many works leveraged deep convolutional layers for feature extraction and affordance localization \cite{chen2023survey .}
elements.sent-2411-033: These deep-learning-based approaches rely on supervised learning, requiring costly annotated training data and often exhibiting poor generalization \cite{nguyen2016detecting, nguyen2017objectbased, do2018affordancenet, fang2020learning .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Earlier advancements in task-aware grasping have focused on integrating task-specific constraints and learning from human demonstrations \cite{saito2022taskgraspinghumandemonstration .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Similarly, \cite{Liu2022 proposed a method combining data-driven grasp constraint learning with one-shot human demonstrations, allowing robots to infer task constraints and reproduce grasping tasks effectively.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Another approach combines a learned geometric grasp model and a CNN-based semantic model to generate grasps that are mechanically fit and contextually compatible with the task \cite{8206162 .}
elements.sent-2411-037: These approaches, however, require expensive annotated data and struggle with generalizing to novel object categories.
elements.sent-2411-038: Large Language Models (LLMs) like GPT-4 \cite{openai2024gpt4technicalreport and Gemini \cite{geminiteam2024geminifamilyhighlycapable have shown impressive abilities in tasks such as test-taking, computer vision, and natural language processing, which made them popular in both research and practical applications.}}
elements.sent-2411-039: These models excel at generalization and few-shot reasoning due to their vast encoded knowledge \cite{brown2020language , which makes them highly suited for robotic applications where adaptability and contextual understanding are crucial \cite{firoozi2023foundationmodelsroboticsapplications .}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Their ability to demonstrate a form of "common sense" enables them to interpret ambiguous scenarios and respond appropriately, offering significant potential for developing models that generalize far more effectively \cite{bommasani2022opportunitiesrisksfoundationmodels .}
elements.sent-2411-041: Despite these promising advantages, utilizing this information in robotics is challenging due to the difficulty of grounding abstract task concepts in physical contexts \cite{liu2024aligningcyberspacephysical, driess2023palmeembodiedmultimodallanguage .}
elements.sent-2411-042: The key challenge lies in bridging the real world with the cyberspace \cite{liu2024aligningcyberspacephysical .}
elements.sent-2411-043: Given these opportunities and challenges, several works have demonstrated the potential of LLMs in grasping \cite{huang2024copageneralroboticmanipulation , manipulation \cite{huang2024manipvqainjectingroboticaffordance , as well as high level planning \cite{driess2023palmeembodiedmultimodallanguage , underscoring their relevance and applicability for task-aware grasping.}}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/pipeline_segmentation.pdf} \caption{The zero-shot semantic fine-grained segmentation is achieved by performing a Principal Component Analysis and rendering the 3D Object along its most variance-explaining axes. The 2D Image is then segmented by SAM \cite{kirillov2023segment} and labeled using a VLM. A projection back to 3D is used to generate the labeled semantic 3D subparts.} \label{fig:segmentation_pipeline}
elements.sent-2411-044: Given the limitations of deep-learning based approaches and the promising qualities of LLMs, \cite{tong2024ovalpromptopenvocabularyaffordancelocalization proposed to ground affordances using Foundation Models and then forwarding the gained insight into a grasp engine.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Their pipeline could show a similar performance to the supervised learning methods \cite{nguyen2016detecting, nguyen2017objectbased, do2018affordancenet, fang2020learning, deng20213daffordancenetbenchmarkvisual .}
elements.sent-2411-046: Significant efforts by \cite{tang2023graspgptleveragingsemanticknowledge, tang2024foundationgraspgeneralizabletaskorientedgrasping also leveraged foundation models to identify grasp points and address how to grasp objects.}
elements.sent-2411-047: However, their methods still rely on extensive manually annotated datasets, which are labor-intensive and costly to create.
elements.sent-2411-048: However, the main focus is on picking up the object and the actual task is not considered in the work.
elements.sent-2411-049: Our approach bridges the gap between high-level task understanding and robotic grasping by introducing a novel method to segment objects into meaningful subparts and give each subart a semantic label.
elements.sent-2411-050: While the semantic label can be understood by an LLM, the physical subpart can be used to find an associated grasp.
elements.sent-2411-051: The vast knowledge and common-sense of LLMs is used to identify the semantic label of the subparts responsible for grasping and task execution.
elements.sent-2411-052: Through the connection between semantic label and physical subpart, the diverse grasp archive generated by QD-Grasp is analyzed and an optimal grasp-pose determined.
elements.sent-2411-053: This direct alignment of LLM-encoded knowledge about grasping locations with the physical representation of the object is to our knowledge unprecedented in literature.
elements.sent-2411-054: By eliminating the need for annotated data, our training-free approach offers a scalable and efficient solution for task-aware grasping.
elements.sent-2411-055: In this section, we outline our methodology for deriving task-conditioned grasp configurations from segmented object point clouds.
elements.sent-2411-056: We start by defining the problem space and identifying task-specific objectives that guide the grasp selection.
elements.sent-2411-057: Building upon this, we introduce a segmentation strategy that combines open-vocabulary models with 3D point cloud projections, followed by a prompt-based approach to identify relevant subparts for grasping and task execution.
elements.sent-2411-058: Finally, we discuss how human judgment is incorporated to establish a ground truth, ensuring that the proposed methodology aligns with human intuition on task-specific grasp preferences.
elements.sent-2411-059: We define the grasp configuration space \( \mathcal{G \) as the set of all possible 6 DoF grasps, where each grasp \( g = (p, R) \) is specified by position \( p \in \mathbb{R ^3 \) and orientation \( R \in SO(3) \).}}
elements.sent-2411-060: Let \( A \subset \mathcal{G \) represent the grasp archive generated by the Quality Diversity algorithm \cite{huber2024speeding6dofgraspsampling .}}
elements.sent-2411-061: Let \( T(g) \) represent the task-specific objective for grasp \( g \).
elements.sent-2411-062: We can formulate the following optimization problem \begin{equation}g^* = \argmin_{g \in A} T(g),\end{equation} where \( g^* \) represents the optimal grasp conditioned on the task.
elements.sent-2411-063: The main challenge is to find a definition of \( T(g) \) such that the optimization problem results in a \( g^* \) that is optimal for the task ahead.
elements.sent-2411-064: An object \( O \) is defined by a combination of a semantic label \( L \) (e.g., "mug") and its geometric properties represented by a point cloud \( \mathcal{P \in \mathbb{R ^{N \times 3 \).}}}
elements.sent-2411-065: Thus, an object is characterized as: \begin{equation}O = (L, P).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-066: For the object \( O \), we aim to generate a set of subparts, denoted as the vocabulary \( V(O) \).
elements.sent-2411-067: Each subpart \( s_i \) within this vocabulary is characterized by a semantic label \( L_i \) and a point cloud \( \mathcal{P _i \) capturing its shape and position within the object.}
elements.sent-2411-068: The vocabulary of subparts is formally defined as: \begin{equation}V(O) = \{ s_i \mid s_i = (L_i, P_i) \}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-069: Performing such a semantic segmentation is not a trivial task.
elements.sent-2411-070: We therefore propose the following steps to generate \( V(O) \).
elements.sent-2411-071: Render and Segment the Point Cloud: Render the 3D point cloud to generate image \( \mathcal{I = \text{render (P) \), which is done by first performing a Principal Component Analysis (PCA) on the point cloud to determine the principal axes.}}
elements.sent-2411-072: The two dimensions with the highest variance are selected for rendering, while the camera is aligned along the dimension with the least variance.
elements.sent-2411-073: Segment the rendered 2D image using SAM \cite{kirillov2023segment to obtain segmented 2D regions.}
elements.sent-2411-074: Generate Candidate Labels: Use a Vision-Language Model (VLM) to generate a set of candidate semantic labels \( \{ L_1, L_2, \ldots, L_n \} \) for object \( O \), based on the object’s overall label \( L \) and the image \( \mathcal{I \).}
elements.sent-2411-075: Assign Labels to 2D Segments: Use a VLM to assign a semantic label \( L_i \) to each segmented 2D region from the set of candidate labels \( \{ L_1, L_2, \ldots, L_n \} \).
elements.sent-2411-076: Project Labeled Segments into 3D: Combine duplicate and remove overlapping regions.
elements.sent-2411-077: Then project the labeled 2D segments back into 3D to obtain the labeled subparts \(s_i = (L_i, \mathcal{P _i) \).}
elements.sent-2411-078: This approach leverages 2D open vocabulary segmentation with SAM and combines it with language models to efficiently identify and label the components of an object in 3D.
elements.sent-2411-079: The segmentation pipeline is illustrated in figure \ref{fig:segmentation_pipeline .}
elements.sent-2411-080: We construct a prompt \( P \) from the task \( \mathcal{T \) and the set of subpart labels \( \{ L_1, L_2, \ldots, L_n \} \).}
elements.sent-2411-081: An LLM is used to identify:.
elements.sent-2411-082: \begin{equation}(L_grasp, L_task) = LLM(P),\end{equation} where the prompt \( P \) is given by: \begin{equation}P = ConstructPrompt(T, \{ L_1, L_2, \ldots, L_n \}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-083: We then use the labels \( L_\text{grasp \) and \( L_\text{task \) to construct the cost function \( T(g) \).}}
elements.sent-2411-084: The idea is to find a grasp in the archive that is both close to the subpart that the LLM proposes to grasp (\( L_\text{grasp \)) as well as far away from the subpart that the LLM proposes will perform the task (\( L_\text{task \)).}}
elements.sent-2411-085: This will punish grasps where the robot's gripper would obstruct the task.
elements.sent-2411-086: In order to do this we calculate the shortest Euclidean distances between the gripper-object contact point \( p_g \) and each subpart’s point cloud \(\mathcal{P _i \): \begin{equation}d_grasp = dist(p_g, P_{L_grasp}), \quad d_task = dist(p_g, P_{L_task}).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-087: Finally, we define \( T(g) \) as: \begin{equation}T(g) = -d_grasp + \alpha \cdot d_task,\end{equation} where \( \alpha \) is a weighting factor that balances the importance of minimizing \( d_\text{grasp \) and maximizing \( d_\text{task \).}}
elements.sent-2411-088: This formulation captures \( T(g) \) as an objective function that incorporates the requirements of the task on the grasp.
elements.sent-2411-089: For details on \( \text{ConstructPrompt (\cdot) \), see the appendix \ref{appendix:system_architecture .}}
elements.sent-2411-090: Figure \ref{fig:full illustrates the described pipeline visually.}
elements.sent-2411-091: Establishing an objective ground truth for task-conditioned grasping is inherently challenging due to the subjective nature of task-specific requirements.
elements.sent-2411-092: Unlike conventional grasp planning metrics, which focus on stability or grasp feasibility, task-aware grasping depends on context-specific human intuition.
elements.sent-2411-093: To address this, we conducted a survey to capture human preferences on where to grasp an object based on the intended task.
elements.sent-2411-094: The collected data offers a robust scientific foundation for evaluating the accuracy and effectiveness of our model's predicted grasps.. Survey Design:.
elements.sent-2411-095: Object and Task Selection: A diverse set of objects with distinct geometric and semantic properties was chosen, paired with tasks that represent common real-world actions (e.g., "pouring" for a mug, "cutting" for a knife).
elements.sent-2411-096: Grasp Selection: Participants were presented with an image of the objects along with descriptions of the associated tasks.
elements.sent-2411-097: They were asked to indicate their preferred grasp region on the object for each task.
elements.sent-2411-098: Data Aggregation: The chosen grasp locations were recorded and aggregated to create probabilistic grasp maps, capturing the popularity of selecting a region for a given task.
elements.sent-2411-099: The survey results provide task-specific grasp regions that align with human intuition.
elements.sent-2411-100: These grasp regions serve as a reference for validating and bench marking our model.
elements.sent-2411-101: By grounding our methodology in human judgment, we bridge the gap between abstract task descriptions and their physical realizations.
elements.sent-2411-102: Figure \ref{fig:ground_truth shows the ground truth regions for a power drill for different subsequent tasks.}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{figures/ground_truth.pdf} \caption{Grasping Regions that were determined in the survey. For each task, the participants had to highlight a region were they would grasp the object in order to do a subsequent task. The figure shows an aggregation of the data that was collected in the survey. The intensity of green indicates the regions popularity for the specific task.} \label{fig:ground_truth}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{figures/robot_experiment.pdf} \caption{The Franka Emika robotic arm, equipped with a Panda 2-DoF gripper, used for conducting experimental evaluations of task-specific grasping. The figure illustrates the progression of two distinct grasps, each shaped by a specific task. The left grasp aligns with the task of filling the cup, while the right one is suited for handing it over. This demonstrates how our model effectively plans grasps while considering the task.} \label{fig:franka}
elements.sent-2411-103: To experimentally validate the task-aware grasping framework, the code was deployed on a Franka Emika Arm with a Panda Gripper as seen in figure \ref{fig:franka .}
elements.sent-2411-104: The full pipeline involves a 6-DoF pose estimation of the object through Megapose \cite{labbé2022megapose6dposeestimation as well as a classification through DETIC \cite{zhou2022detectingtwentythousandclassesusing .}}
elements.sent-2411-105: After classifying the object in the workspace, the system prompts the user to specify the task.
elements.sent-2411-106: The presented framework determines the task and grasp part which are used to create the cost function \( T(g) \) as described in the section \ref{subsec:task_condition .}
elements.sent-2411-107: The pre-computed grasp-archives are loaded and evaluated which results in an optimal grasp \( g^* \).
elements.sent-2411-108: Through move it motion planning \cite{coleman2014reducingbarrierentrycomplex , the inverse kinematics are calculated and the grasp is executed.}
elements.sent-2411-109: We chose {\tt GPT-4o both as the VLM in the segmentation pipeline as well as the LLM for the grasp region suggestions.}
elements.sent-2411-110: The method was evaluated using a subset of the YCB dataset \cite{ycb_pub1,ycb_pub2,ycb_pub3 , selecting objects that encompass a broad range of geometries and visual appearances.}
elements.sent-2411-111: Particular preference was given to objects with multiple affordances, especially those where the grasp location would vary depending on the intended task.
elements.sent-2411-112: This selection enables us to test the framework's ability to adapt to task-specific grasping requirements.
elements.sent-2411-113: A comprehensive list of the selected objects and the tested tasks is provided in Appendix \ref{appendix:dataset .}
elements.sent-2411-114: The segmentation pipeline demonstrates promising results by effectively distinguishing fine-grained subparts of objects in open-vocabulary scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-115: Leveraging the combination of SAM \cite{kirillov2023segment and large-scale vision-language models such as GPT-4 \cite{openai2024gpt4technicalreport , our pipeline generates semantic labels for subparts that are visually meaningful and contextually appropriate.}}
elements.sent-2411-116: However, evaluating the accuracy of these segmentations remains a challenging task due to the open nature of both the objects and the vocabulary used.
elements.sent-2411-117: Unlike fixed-category segmentation problems, our method is designed to handle arbitrary object classes and corresponding labels, making quantitative evaluation difficult in the absence of a predefined ground truth.
elements.sent-2411-118: Despite these challenges, qualitative assessments indicate that the proposed pipeline aligns well with human intuition, producing segmentations that are coherent with the objects' geometric and semantic characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-119: Figure \ref{fig:segmentations shows a selection of segmentations that have been performed on objects from the YCB dataset.}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{figures/segmentation_result.pdf} \caption{Examples of labeled segmentation masks generated by the segmentation pipeline.} \label{fig:segmentations}
elements.sent-2411-120: Grasp regions are compared with the ground truth by a weighted intersection over union.
elements.sent-2411-121: The weights are determined by the overlap between the different answers of the participants in the survey.
elements.sent-2411-122: Our model reached an IoU of 76.4 \% with a precision of 91.1 \% and a recall pf 76.4 \%.
elements.sent-2411-123: The proposed approach demonstrates a promising direction in integrating semantic understanding and geometric reasoning for task-aware grasping.
elements.sent-2411-124: By leveraging vision-language models and open-vocabulary segmentation, we introduce a structured representation that aligns an LLM’s encoded task knowledge with a robot’s grasping capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-125: However, several limitations remain, particularly concerning grasp orientation and applicability of our method.
elements.sent-2411-126: One significant limitation is that our approach does not explicitly consider the orientation of the gripper during grasping.
elements.sent-2411-127: As a result, while the selected grasp point may be on the correct subpart, the orientation may not be optimal for performing the subsequent task.
elements.sent-2411-128: For example, grasping a mug’s handle to pour might require a specific angle, which is not captured in our current formulation of the task-conditioned objective \( T(g) \).
elements.sent-2411-129: Addressing this would require extending the objective to incorporate orientation constraints that align with task requirements.
elements.sent-2411-130: Another limitation is that our framework is currently designed for antipodal grippers, which are simpler in their mechanics and degrees of freedom.
elements.sent-2411-131: If more sophisticated, multi-fingered robotic hands are used, the definition and evaluation of grasps would need to be significantly refined.
elements.sent-2411-132: This would involve considering additional variables, such as finger positioning, contact forces, and more complex hand kinematics, which our current approach does not yet address.
elements.sent-2411-133: A further significant limitation of the proposed approach is its difficulty in effectively segmenting concave or complex-shaped objects.
elements.sent-2411-134: The segmentation pipeline relies heavily on Principal Component Analysis (PCA) to render 3D objects into 2D projections, which are then segmented using vision-language models.
elements.sent-2411-135: However, for objects with concave or complex geometries, the PCA-based rendering might fail to project subparts onto a 2D image in a manner that preserves their semantic boundaries.
elements.sent-2411-136: As a result, the segmentation algorithm would struggle to correctly identify and label distinct subparts of such objects.
elements.sent-2411-137: This occurs because PCA projections for concave or intricate shapes may obscure important details or merge separate subparts into a single region, leading to incomplete or inaccurate segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-138: Addressing this limitation would require a more sophisticated rendering technique that maintains semantic information even in the presence of challenging object geometries.
elements.sent-2411-139: This works takes a high quality 3D Scan of the object as an input which is required for the segmentation and grasp region identification.
elements.sent-2411-140: In a more applied real world scenario, it would be more likely to have an RGBD scan of the scene as input.
elements.sent-2411-141: The concepts presented in this work are also applicable to a RGBD Scan which could be of interest for future research.
elements.sent-2411-142: This work presents a zero-shot, task-aware robotic grasping framework that integrates semantic understanding with geometric reasoning, enabling robots to select grasps aligned with task requirements without any additional training.
elements.sent-2411-143: By leveraging vision-language models and a custom segmentation pipeline, we achieved a strong performance, with a 76.4 IoU compared to the human ground truth, demonstrating the effectiveness of our approach in identifying task-specific grasp regions.
elements.sent-2411-144: While the framework shows great promise, challenges remain in handling complex object geometries with different grippers and incorporating orientation control for more nuanced grasp tasks.
elements.sent-2411-145: Future work should focus on improving segmentation for concave shapes and refining orientation adjustments to further enhance task-oriented grasp precision.
elements.sent-2411-146: This work was supported by the European Union Horizon Europe Framework Program, through the PILLAR (grant agreement 101070381) and euROBIN (grant agreement 101070596) projects.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran \appendices.}
elements.sent-2411-147: The model invokes the VLM / LLM a total of three times.
elements.sent-2411-148: The first time to generate a list of candidate subparts, secondly to label each mask generated by SAM \cite{kirillov2023segment and finally to predict which part to grasp and which is responsible for the task execution.}
elements.sent-2411-149: The following listings show the system prompts and user prompts for the three usages of the LLM.
elements.sent-2411-150: The interaction between the LLM and the framework goes through python snippets that are extracted from the natural language response.
elements.sent-2411-151: The colored keywords are replaced with variables that change dependent on task and object.

elements.sent-2411-152: The model was tested on a diverse subset of the YCB dataset \cite{ycb_pub1,ycb_pub2,ycb_pub3 .}
elements.sent-2411-153: The objects were chosen such that they would need to be grasped differently in order to fulfill different tasks.
elements.sent-2411-154: In a second step, for each object, a set of tasks were chosen that would be evaluated.

elements.sent-2411-156: The relevant subparts for grasping and task execution are determined by prompting a Large Language Model (LLM).
elements.sent-2411-157: Finally, Quality Diversity algorithms generate grasp candidates for the object.
elements.sent-2411-158: The ideal grasp for a given task is found by maximizing a score function that rewards grasping on the suggested grasp subpart, maintaining a distance from the task subpart, while encouraging a high grasp force.. \end{strip.}
elements.sent-2411-159: This paper proposes a novel framework that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) and Quality Diversity (QD) algorithms to enable zero-shot task-conditioned grasp synthesis.
elements.sent-2411-160: We evaluated the proposed method on a subset of the YCB dataset with a Franka Emika robot.
elements.sent-2411-161: A consolidated ground truth for task-specific grasp regions is established through a survey.
elements.sent-2411-162: Our work achieves a weighted intersection over union (IoU) of 73.6\% in predicting task-conditioned grasp regions in 65 task-object combinations.
elements.sent-2411-163: An end-to-end validation study on a smaller subset further confirms the effectiveness of our approach, with 88\% of responses favoring the task-aware grasp over the control group.
elements.sent-2411-164: A binomial test shows that participants significantly prefer the task-aware grasp.
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/pipeline_segmentation.pdf} \caption{The zero-shot semantic fine-grained segmentation is achieved by performing a Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and rendering the 3D Object along its most variance-explaining axes. The 2D Image is then segmented by {\tt segment-anything} \cite{kirillov2023segment} and labeled using {\tt GPT-4o} \cite{openai2024gpt4technicalreport} as a Vision Language Model (VLM). A projection back to 3D is used to generate the labeled semantic 3D subparts.} \label{fig:segmentation_pipeline}
elements.sent-2411-165: The grasp chosen for an object is influenced not only by its geometric properties but also by its functional requirements and the semantic understanding of its purpose.
elements.sent-2411-166: Traditional grasp planning approaches often aim to identify stable or feasible grasps, largely independently from the object's broader context or its intended use \cite{Kleeberger2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-167: This limitation creates a gap between robotic grasping capabilities and the precise demands of real-world applications, where the appropriateness of a grasp is inherently tied to the task that follows, such as pouring liquid from a cup, using a tool for assembly, or positioning an object for inspection.
elements.sent-2411-168: Addressing these dependencies is critical to improving task-oriented robotic manipulation.
elements.sent-2411-169: The concept of affordances was introduced by Gibson in 1979 \cite{gibson1979ecological and lays a theoretical foundation in robotics for bridging perception with action, allowing robots to understand the functional properties of objects beyond their geometric or visual characteristics.}
elements.sent-2411-170: This theoretical basis inspired early task-oriented grasping approaches like \cite{fang2018learningtaskorientedgraspingtool and \cite{chen2022learning6doftaskorientedgrasp that can generate task-aware grasping poses using supervised learning methods.}}
elements.sent-2411-171: Unfortunately, these methods require expensive annotated data and struggle with generalizing to unseen object categories.
elements.sent-2411-172: Large language models (LLMs) such as {\tt GPT-4o \cite{openai2024gpt4technicalreport have shown impressive abilities in tasks such as test-taking, computer vision, and natural language processing, which made them popular in both research and practical applications.}}
elements.sent-2411-173: LLMs excel in generalization and few-shot reasoning due to their vast encoded knowledge \cite{brown2020language , making them highly suited for robotic applications where adaptability and contextual understanding are crucial \cite{firoozi2023foundationmodelsroboticsapplications .}}
elements.sent-2411-174: Their ability to demonstrate a form of ``common sense'' enables them to interpret ambiguous scenarios and respond appropriately, offering significant potential for developing models that generalize far more effectively \cite{bommasani2022opportunitiesrisksfoundationmodels .}
elements.sent-2411-175: Despite these promising advantages, the use of this information in robotics is challenging due to the difficulty in grounding abstract task concepts in physical contexts \cite{liu2024aligningcyberspacephysical .}
elements.sent-2411-176: Given these opportunities and challenges, several works have demonstrated the potential of LLMs in grasping \cite{huang2024copageneralroboticmanipulation and manipulation \cite{huang2024manipvqainjectingroboticaffordance , underscoring their relevance and applicability for task-aware grasping.}}
elements.sent-2411-177: Given the limitations of supervised learning approaches and the promising qualities of LLMs, \cite{tong2024ovalpromptopenvocabularyaffordancelocalization proposed to ground affordances using foundation models and then forward the gained insight into a grasp engine.}
elements.sent-2411-178: Significant efforts by \cite{tang2023graspgptleveragingsemanticknowledge, tang2024foundationgraspgeneralizabletaskorientedgrasping leveraged foundation models to identify both where and how to grasp objects.}
elements.sent-2411-179: However, these methods still depend on manually annotated expensive datasets.
elements.sent-2411-180: QD-Grasp \cite{huber2023qualitydiversitysparsereward is a novel approach to grasp synthesis that uses quality diversity algorithms to generate a diverse set of stable and high-quality grasps.}
elements.sent-2411-181: By simultaneously optimizing for diversity and stability, QD-Grasp produces an extensive archive of distinct yet reliable grasp poses.
elements.sent-2411-182: This approach enables efficient sampling of a large number of grasps, facilitating the creation of comprehensive datasets suitable for policy learning \cite{huber2024qdgsetlargescalegrasping .}
elements.sent-2411-183: Considering that grasping is inherently linked to the subsequent task, the incorporation of task awareness into grasp synthesis is therefore essential to fully leverage the potential of QD-Grasp.
elements.sent-2411-184: Grasp archives should not only be varied and robust, but also contextually relevant.
elements.sent-2411-185: Our approach bridges the gap between high-level task understanding and robotic grasping by introducing a novel method to segment objects into subparts and give each subpart a semantic label.
elements.sent-2411-186: LLMs can identify the subpart to be grasped based on the task, which is linked to physical regions through the segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-187: By analyzing the QD generated grasp archive we can determine the optimal task-aware grasp pose.
elements.sent-2411-188: This direct alignment of LLM-encoded knowledge about grasping locations with the physical representation of the object is, to our knowledge, unprecedented in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-189: Our training-free approach provides a scalable and efficient way to generate task-aware grasps without requiring any annotated data.
elements.sent-2411-190: This section presents our methodology for leveraging LLMs to condition grasps on a given task.
elements.sent-2411-191: We propose a novel open-vocabulary 3D segmentation method that we use to identify task-relevant subparts by prompting an LLM.
elements.sent-2411-192: We define the grasp configuration space \( \mathcal{G \) as the set of all possible 6 degree-of-freedom (DoF) grasps, where each grasp \( g = (p, R) \) is specified by position \( p \in \mathbb{R ^3 \) and orientation \( R \in SO(3) \).}}
elements.sent-2411-193: Let \( C(g, \mathcal{T ) \) represent the task-compatibility score for grasp \( g \) and task \( \mathcal{T \).}}
elements.sent-2411-194: We want to design \( C(g, \mathcal{T ) \) so that it captures the requirements of the tasks and ranks the grasps according to their compatibility with the task \(\mathcal{T}\).}
elements.sent-2411-195: With the defined function \( C(g, \mathcal{T ) \), the optimal task-aware grasp \( g^* \) can be found through an optimization problem: \begin{equation}g^* = \argmax_{g \in A} C(g,T),\end{equation} The goal is to define \( C(g, \mathcal{T ) \) in a way that ensures the optimization yields an optimal grasp \( g^* \).}}
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{figures/segmentation_results.pdf} \caption{Examples of labeled segmentation masks generated by the grasp segmentation pipeline using {\tt GPT-4o} \cite{openai2024gpt4technicalreport} as the VLM.} \label{fig:segmentations}
elements.sent-2411-196: An object \( O \) is defined by a combination of a semantic label \( L \) (for example, ``mug'') and its geometric properties represented by a point cloud \( \mathcal{P \in \mathbb{R ^{N \times 3 \).}}}
elements.sent-2411-197: For the object \( O \), our objective is to generate a set of subparts, denoted as the vocabulary \( V(O) \).
elements.sent-2411-198: Each subpart \( s_i \) within this vocabulary is characterized by a semantic label \( L_i \) and a point cloud \( \mathcal{P _i \) that captures its shape and relative pose within the object.}
elements.sent-2411-199: The vocabulary is defined as: \begin{equation}V(O) = \{ s_i \mid s_i = (L_i, P_i) \}, where \quad \bigcup_i P_i = P \quad and \quad P_i \cap P_j = \emptyset, \; i \neq j.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-009: \begin{equation}V(O) = \{ s_i \mid s_i = (L_i, P_i) \}, where \quad \bigcup_i P_i = P \quad and \quad P_i \cap P_j = \emptyset, \; i \neq j.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-200: The vocabulary \( V(O) \) is generated by performing a semantic segmentation on the object \( O \).
elements.sent-2411-201: As open-vocabulary semantic segmentation on 3D parts is unsolved and highly challenging \cite{xiao20243dopenvocabularypanopticsegmentation , we propose the following method.}
elements.sent-2411-202: Render and Segment the Point Cloud: Render the 3D point cloud to generate image \( \mathcal{I = \text{render (P) \).}}
elements.sent-2411-203: This is done by performing a Principal Component Analysis (PCA) on the point cloud to determine the object viewing angle: the two dimensions with the highest variance are selected for rendering, while the camera is aligned along the dimension with the least variance.
elements.sent-2411-204: Furthermore, the rendered 2D image is segmented using {\tt segment-anything \cite{kirillov2023segment to obtain 2D masks.}}
elements.sent-2411-205: Generate Candidate Labels: Use a Vision-Language Model (VLM) such as {\tt GPT-4o \cite{openai2024gpt4technicalreport to generate a set of candidate semantic labels \( \{ L_1, L_2, \ldots, L_n \} \) for the object \( O \), based on the overall label of the object \( L \) and the image \( \mathcal{I \).}}}
elements.sent-2411-206: As we intend to find graspable and task-relevant subparts, we specify in the VLM prompt that the candidate labels should be graspable or task-relevant subparts of the object \( O \).
elements.sent-2411-207: Project Labeled Segments into 3D: Combine duplicate and remove overlapping regions, then project the labeled 2D segments back into 3D to obtain the labeled subparts \(s_i = (L_i, \mathcal{P _i) \) and, thus, the vocabulary \(V(O)\).}
elements.sent-2411-208: This approach leverages 2D segmentation with {\tt segment-anything}\cite{kirillov2023segment and combines it with VLMs to efficiently identify and label the components of an object in 3D.}
elements.sent-2411-209: We construct a prompt \( P \) for the task \( \mathcal{T \) and the set of subpart labels \( \{ L_1, L_2, \ldots, L_n \} \).}
elements.sent-2411-210: The prompt \(P\) asks the LLM to return the following two labels:.
elements.sent-2411-211: \begin{equation}(L_grasp, L_task) = LLM(P),\end{equation} The provided labels \( L_\text{grasp \) and \( L_\text{task \) are used to construct the score function \( C(g,\mathcal{T ) \).}}}
elements.sent-2411-212: We intend to minimize the chance of the gripper interfering with the task by rewarding grasps with a large distance between the grasp points and the task subpart \(L_\text{task}\).
elements.sent-2411-213: We therefore define \(d_\text{task}\) as the minimal euclidean distance between the gripper-object contact point \( p_g \) and the subpart point cloud \(\mathcal{P _{L_\text{task \). \begin{equation}d_{task} = \min_{p \in P_{L_{task}}} \| p_g - p \|_2\end{equation} Additionally, we determine the grasped subpart \(L_{g}\) of grasp \(g\) by finding the label of the point cloud closest to the grasp point \(p_g\): \begin{equation}L_g = L_i \quad where \quad i = \argmin_{j \in \{ j' \mid (L_{j'}, P_{j'}) \in V(O) \}} \| p_g - P_j \|_2\end{equation} Finally, we define the score function \( C(g,\mathcal{T ) \) as: \begin{equation}\fbox{ $ C(g,T) =  K_force \cdot F + K_dist \cdot d_task, if L_g = L_grasp 0, otherwise  $ }\end{equation} where \(F\) is the contact force, that we obtain from the pybullet simulation engine used to generate the QD grasp archive.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-011: \begin{equation}L_g = L_i \quad where \quad i = \argmin_{j \in \{ j' \mid (L_{j'}, P_{j'}) \in V(O) \}} \| p_g - P_j \|_2\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-012: \begin{equation}\fbox{ $ C(g,T) =  K_force \cdot F + K_dist \cdot d_task, if L_g = L_grasp 0, otherwise  $ }\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-214: This formulation defines \( C(g,\mathcal{T ) \) as a score function that, when maximized, discards all grasps not on \( L_\text{grasp \) while encouraging strong grasps with distance to \( L_\text{task \), therefore giving us the task-conditioned grasp \(g^*\). \begin{equation}g^* = \argmax_{g \in A} C(g,T)\end{equation} Figure \ref{fig:full provides a visual overview of the described pipeline.}}}}
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{figures/robot_experiment.pdf} \caption{The Franka Emika robotic arm, equipped with a Panda 2-DoF gripper, used for conducting experimental evaluations of task-specific grasping. The figure illustrates the progression of two distinct grasps, each conditioned on a separate task. The left grasp is for the task of cutting something with the knife, and the right one is for handing the knife over to someone.} \label{fig:franka}
elements.sent-2411-215: To experimentally validate the task-aware grasping framework, the method was deployed on a Franka Emika arm with a Panda 2-DoF gripper as seen in Figure \ref{fig:franka .}
elements.sent-2411-216: The system asks the user to specify the task \(\mathcal{T}\).
elements.sent-2411-217: The presented framework determines the task part \( L_\text{task \) and grasp part \( L_\text{grasp \) which are used to create the score function \( C(g,\mathcal{T ) \) as described in section \ref{subsec:task_condition .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-218: The precomputed grasp archive \(A\) is loaded and evaluated, resulting in an optimal grasp \( g^* \).
elements.sent-2411-219: Through \(\tt{Moveit!}\) \cite{coleman2014reducingbarrierentrycomplex motion planning, the approach trajectory is computed and the grasp is executed.}
elements.sent-2411-220: We chose {\tt GPT-4o \cite{openai2024gpt4technicalreport as both the VLM in the segmentation pipeline and the LLM for the grasp region suggestions.}}
elements.sent-2411-221: All the following results have been produced with this set-up.
elements.sent-2411-222: The method was evaluated using a subset of the {\tt YCB dataset \cite{ycb_pub , selecting objects that encompass a wide range of geometries and visual appearances.}}
elements.sent-2411-223: We defined a large set \(S_\text{large}\) of 65 task-object combinations for the validation of the grasp region prediction, described in section \ref{results:grasp_region_prediction , as well as a small set \(S_\text{small}\) of 7 task-object combinations for end-to-end validation on the real robot, as described in section \ref{results:end_to_end_validation .}}
elements.sent-2411-224: Table \ref{tab:dataset shows the combination of tasks and objects, where objects and tasks from \(S_\text{small}\) are colored in red.}
elements.figure-2411-009: \begin{tabular}{|m{0.12\textwidth}|m{0.3\textwidth}|} Name Tasks \texttt{\textcolor{red}{screwdriver}} hand over, place, insert, stir, shake, \textcolor{red}{screw}, prick powerdrill hand over, place, shake, screw bowl hand over, place, pour, scoop, fill \texttt{\textcolor{red}{pitcher}} hand over, place, pour, shake, \textcolor{red}{fill} \texttt{\textcolor{red}{spatula}} hand over, place, hang, stir, shake, \textcolor{red}{flip} fork hand over, place, cut, prick spoon hand over, place, stir, scoop, fill \texttt{\textcolor{red}{scissors}} hand over, place, cut, insert, \textcolor{red}{hang} hammer hand over, place, press, hammer marker hand over, place, insert, squeeze, write \texttt{\textcolor{red}{mug}} hand over, place, hang, pour, shake, scoop, \textcolor{red}{fill} \texttt{\textcolor{red}{knife}} \textcolor{red}{hand over}, place, \textcolor{red}{cut}, stir, shake, prick \end{tabular} \caption{A comprehensive list of the task-object combinations of \(S_\text{large}\). The subset \(S_\text{small}\) is indicated in \textcolor{red}{red}.} \label{tab:dataset}
elements.sent-2411-225: However, evaluating the accuracy of these segmentation masks is a challenging task due to the open nature of both the objects and the vocabulary used.
elements.sent-2411-226: Unlike fixed-category segmentation problems, our method is designed to handle arbitrary object classes and corresponding labels, making quantitative evaluation difficult.
elements.sent-2411-227: However, qualitative assessments suggest that the proposed pipeline generates segmentation masks that are useful for task-aware grasping, as shown in Figure \ref{fig:segmentations .}
elements.sent-2411-228: Establishing a ground truth for task-conditioned grasping is challenging due to the subjective nature of task-specific requirements.
elements.sent-2411-229: Unlike conventional grasp planning metrics, which focus on stability or feasibility, task-aware grasping lacks a universally accepted evaluation method.
elements.sent-2411-230: While task performance could serve as an alternative metric, directly evaluating grasps through real-world task execution is often impractical.
elements.sent-2411-231: Given this constraint, we use human intuition as an alternative reference.
elements.sent-2411-232: To capture human preferences, we conducted a survey on where to grasp an object based on the intended task.
elements.sent-2411-233: For every task-object combination in \(S_\text{large}\), we asked six participants to indicate their preferred grasp region, which we then combined into a consolidated ground truth.
elements.sent-2411-234: Figure \ref{fig:ground_truth shows the ground truth for a power drill for different subsequent tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-235: When inferring the model for a certain task \(\mathcal{T}\), the label \(L_{\text{grasp }\) is generated, which is coupled to a geometric subpart \(\mathcal{P _{\text{grasp }\) on the object through the vocabulary \(V(O)\).}}}
elements.sent-2411-236: We compare this predicted grasp region with the consolidated ground truth by performing a weighted IoU.
elements.sent-2411-237: Weights are allocated based on the extent of overlap in participants' survey responses, where increased overlap signifies greater significance in the consolidated ground truth.
elements.sent-2411-238: Our model reached a weighted IoU of 73.6 \% with a precision of 91.1 \% and a recall of 73.6 \% in the data set \(S_\text{large}\).
elements.sent-2411-239: To ensure the robustness of these metrics and account for potential variations across runs, we repeated the experiment ten times and report the average performance across these runs.
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{figures/ground_truth.pdf} \caption{Consolidated ground truth of grasping regions that were determined in the survey. For each task, the participants had to highlight a region were they would grasp the object in order to do a subsequent task. The figure shows an aggregation of the data that was collected. The intensity of green indicates the regions popularity for the specific task.} \label{fig:ground_truth}
elements.sent-2411-240: A complete end-to-end validation was performed on \(S_\text{small}\), evaluating the full pipeline from task-object combination as input to task-conditioned grasp selection and human preference assessment as output.
elements.sent-2411-241: Similarly to the validation in section \ref{results:grasp_region_prediction , the end-to-end validation also relies on human intuition, where no objective ground truth dataset is available.}
elements.sent-2411-242: To validate the effectiveness of our approach, we determined the optimal task-aware grasp \(g^*\) according to the proposed approach and for each task-object combination \begin{equation}g^* = \argmax_{g \in A} C(g,T), \quad with  K_force = 10, K_dist = 1\end{equation} and sampled three additional grasps \( g_1, g_2, g_3\) for the control group.. \begin{equation}\{ g_1, g_2, g_3 \}  such that  C(g_i,T) = 0, \quad \forall i \in \{1,2,3\}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-015: \begin{equation}\{ g_1, g_2, g_3 \}  such that  C(g_i,T) = 0, \quad \forall i \in \{1,2,3\}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-243: We filmed these grasps and asked a group of participants to select their preferred grasp given the task \(\mathcal{T}\).
elements.sent-2411-244: They could choose from \(g^*, g_1, g_2, g_3\), or \(\tt{none}\).
elements.sent-2411-245: We hypothesize that they will align with the algorithm by favoring the task-aware grasp \(g^*\) over the alternatives.
elements.sent-2411-246: A total of 55 people participated in the survey, with 88\% preferring the task-aware grasp, as shown in Figure \ref{fig:boxplot .}
elements.sent-2411-247: A binomial test, based on the null hypothesis that participants choose each grasp equally often, confirms that the task-aware grasp is significantly preferred (\(\mathbf{p < 0.001}\)).
elements.sent-2411-248: Our approach introduces a new method for task-aware grasping by conditioning 6-DoF grasps on task requirements.
elements.sent-2411-249: By using a custom segmentation method, we can align the LLMs task knowledge with the grasping capabilities of the robot.
elements.sent-2411-250: However, several limitations persist, presenting opportunities for further research.
elements.sent-2411-251: The PCA-based projection in the segmentation pipeline is a key limitation, making it difficult to segment concave or complex-shaped objects.
elements.sent-2411-252: For such geometries, the 2D projection may obscure details or merge distinct subparts, leading to inaccurate segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-253: Since the grasping of such objects itself remains a challenging and partially unsolved problem, this limitation is not overly restrictive.
elements.sent-2411-254: Advances in computer vision are ongoing, and even more effective segmentation methods will emerge in the future.
elements.sent-2411-255: Nonetheless, the segmentation pipeline should be evaluated against an established segmentation dataset to assess its effectiveness with more rigour.
elements.sent-2411-256: While considering the subparts of the objects for grasping is a strong first step, there is significant potential for improvement in the LLM-based grasp specification.
elements.sent-2411-257: For instance, the LLM could be prompted to determine whether a task requires a high or low grasp force, or even to reason about the optimal grasp orientation.
elements.sent-2411-258: Grounding orientation and incorporating it into the LLM’s reasoning could further enhance task-specific grasp selection.
elements.sent-2411-259: All of these ideas could be seamlessly integrated into the task-conditioned score function \( C(g,\mathcal{T ) \), leading to a more adaptive framework that better captures task-specific grasp requirements.}
elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{figures/boxplot.pdf} \caption{Box plot showing participant selection percentages of the survey. The results show strong evidence that the task-awareness algorithm predicts grasps that are aligned with human intuition. } \label{fig:boxplot}
elements.sent-2411-260: Our grasp region prediction achieved strong performance, with 73.6\% IoU compared to a survey-based ground truth.
elements.sent-2411-261: Furthermore, in an end-to-end setting, a binomial test with \(\mathbf{p < 0.001}\) showed that participants significantly preferred the task-aware grasp.
elements.sent-2411-262: Future research should focus on refining segmentation for complex geometries, enhancing LLM interaction, and scaling to large datasets, as addressing these challenges will further strengthen the capabilities of task-aware grasping.
elements.sent-2411-263: Ultimately, this work takes an important step toward solving task-aware grasping, a fundamental aspect of robotic manipulation.
elements.sent-2411-264: This work was supported by the French Agence Nationale de la Recherche (ANR) (ANR-21-FAI1-0004) (Learn2Grasp) and the European Union Horizon Europe Framework Program, through the PILLAR (grant agreement 101070381) and euROBIN (grant agreement 101070596) projects.. { \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}}
